office:text.text:p: Composite Text
5.igi-ni ma-an-Ñar-ma
12.nam-ra-ak-ne-ne uru-uru-bi durun-ù-dè
26.ma-da-ni-ta ga-àm-ta-an-gub-bu
39.ki-ni-åè ba-an-gur-ru-uå
47.za-pa-áÑ-Ñá-ni im-ma-dugud
49.lú tab-ba nu-tuku lú nu-mu-un-da-sá-e
1 Speak to Ibbi-Sin, my king, 2 saying (the words of) Puzur-Numušda, governor of Kazallu, your ­servant:

text:p.text:span: The envoy of Iåbi-Erra came before me (and said):
“My king Iåbi-Erra, ­has sent me to you to you with a message.”
He presented the matter as follows:
“My master Enlil has­ promised me stewardship of the homeland.
Yes, it was me that Enlil ordered
the banks of the Tigris and Euphrates, the Abgal ­and Me-Enlila canals,
their cities, their (city) gods and­ their armies,
from the territory of Hamazi to the Magan Sea, ­
and having made Isin the storehouse of Enlil, made it famous,

text:note-body.text:p: Alt. transl.: Isin, which has to be made the storehouse of Enlil and which has to be made famous.

text:p.text:span: to (then) settle their war captives in all those cities.
I have­ sworn by my personal god Dagan

text:note-body.text:p: Var. I have­ sworn by the name of Enlil, my lord, and of Dagan, my personal god.

text:p.text:span: Because Enlil has promised ­me the cities of the territory,
I shall build all their (i.e. the city gods) shrines in Isin,
I shall celebrate their­ regular festivals;
I shall set up my own statues, ­my own emblems, (choose by omen) my own high priests, chief priests, and high priestesses in their special abodes,
so that my subjects may offer their prayers,
before Enlil in the Ekur (in Nippur)
and before Nanna ­in the EkiånuÑal (in Ur).
And as for you, I shall chase out the person you depend on (­Ibbi-Sin)
It was just as he had predicted:
he took­ over Nippur, appointed his own guard over it,
and ­arrested NiÑ-dugani, the chief temple administrator of Nippur.
(His ally), the ruler/governor­ Zinnum, took prisoners in

text:note-body.text:p: Var. … ­captured NiÑ-dugani, the chief temple administrator of Nippur, and plundered Hamazi. (His ally)­, the ruler/governor Zinnum, took prisoners in Subir.

text:p.text:span: and Puzur-Tutu, the governor of Borsippa
His clamor shakes the frontier territory like a reed fence,

text:note-body.text:p: Var. The frontier territory trembles like a reed fence from his clamor.

text:p.text:span: as Iåbi-Erra­ goes everywhere at the head of the troops.

office:text.text:p:
42 It was just as he had predicted: 43 he captured the­ banks of the Tigris, the banks of the Euphrates, as well as the banks of the Abgal and Me-Enlila. ­44 He installed Idi in Malgium,8
Alt. transl.: He installed Iddin-Malgium.­ 45–46 and when Girbubu, governor of Girkal, resisted him and cut off his (Iddi’s) shield, he (Išbi-Erra) took him prisoner. 47 His­ clamor has become louder, 48 (and now) he has turned his attention in my direction. 49 I have no ally, no one who can match him! ­50 Although he has not yet been able to defeat me, 51 when­ he finally strikes, I will have to flee! Now my king is informed (about all of this)!
Commentary

text:p.text:span: Although this letter is fairly well documented, there are many variants, and it is clear that some scribes did not understand certain lines and attempted to make their own sense of the text. There are eight Nippur manuscripts and these are relatively consistent. The clusters of variants between the Ur and Sippar exemplars and those from unknown sites may be of significance. Of the three unprovenienced texts, X1 is in almost total harmony with the Sippar tablet Si1, and X3 agrees in almost all cases with Ur2. See p.
above. Note that the composition was also known at Uruk. The

office:text.text:p: Perhaps the compiler of this catalog was aware of two different recensions of this epistle; it is also possible that he had access to a completely different letter to the last king of Ur, one that has not yet been recovered.

4–5. The order of these lines consistently differs in Nippur manuscripts and in all other sources. It is clear that some scribes understood the awkward nature of the Nippur order and modified it, moving line 5 after line 3, and including it in the initial statement of Puzur-Numušda. X1 even added a clarifying line after line 5: “Thus speaks my king Išbi-Erra:” It seems that outside of Nippur the letter began:
1 Speak to Ibbi-Sin, my king, 2 saying (the words of) Puzur-Numušda, governor of Kazallu, your servant:

3 The envoy of Iåbi-Erra came before me 5 (and) presented the matter as follows: 4 “My king Iåbi-Erra, ­has sent me to you to you with a message. Thus speaks my king Išbi-Erra:” 6 ‘My master Enlil has­ promised to make me shepherd of the land …’.”

text:p.text:span: . Note the distribution of variants in the writing of the name of the Euphrates in these lines. This is not apparently in line 7 because of the state of preservation of the witnesses but in line 43 the Nippur texts have
), while all the other sources, with the exeption of X1 write
in this name, see, in general, C. Woods,
. On the possible location of Hamazi south of the Lower Zab, see Appendix D.
. The present translation takes into account the use of
in the predicates of this line in the Nippur sources, as opposed to
of the surrounding lines. This distinction
) has been elucidated by Fumi Karahashi and is followed here.

text:note-body.text:p: “Nonfinite Relative Clauses in Gude Cylinder B, Revisited,” paper presented at the 55th Rencontre Assyriologique Internationale, Paris, July 8, 2009. I am grateful to Fumi Karahashi for providing me with a copy of her presentation.

text:p.text:span: This subtle morphological and syntactic distinction was unknown, or was ignored by the author of Ur2; in X1 the first verb has
(in all other sources the verbs are broken). Therefore, in Ur2, and perhaps in other manuscripts, this line should be translated as “to make Isin the storehouse of Enlil, and make it famous.”
. The translation “Yes, …” attempts to render into English the emphatic force of the independent pronoun
, as “to be hostile, aggressive,” follows M. Jaques,
153 n. 345. It is possibly the opposite of
, “to submit,” and therefore the line may have to be translated as “why are you unsubmissive to me?”

office:text.text:p: 15. In all the Nippur sources and the Sippar manuscript (N5 is very worn here and somewhat uncertain), Iåbi-Erra invokes his personal god Dagan. This is undoubtedly meant to underline his foreign origins. The texts Ur2 and X3, which are so often in harmony, add the figure of the main god of Sumer, Enlil, as­ his master. All discussions of this line cite the fuller version. Note the passage in the royal inscription Iddin-Dagan 3: 36–37 (RIME E.4.1.3.3, in curse formula): lú-ba den-líl lugal-Ñu10 dnin-líl ereå-Ñu10 dda-gan diÑir-Ñá nam ha-ba-an-da-ku5-ru-ne, “May Enlil, my king, Ninlil, my queen, and Dagan, my personal god, curse him.” It may be that the tradition of Ur2 and X3 was contaminated by the memory of some Išme-Dagan text, perhaps reinforced by contamination from line 6 above.

text:p.text:span: . Tablets N1 and S1 write the bound ­pronoun on the direct object as
, found in texts from other sites (Ur2, X1, X3), is probably more correct. The idiomatic predicate
here and in line 50 is a calque from Akkadian

, “to conquer.” It is otherwise attested, as far as I know, only in Larsa and Babylon I royal inscriptions (Kudur-mabuk 2 13 [E4.2.13]; Rim-Sin 37 ii 9’ [E4.2.14.14]; Rim-Sin 12 28 [E4.2.14.5]; Samsu-iluna D 33 [E4.3.7.8]); see also IBPu1: 31 (24).
. The only complete version of this line is in text Ur2; manuscript X3 was similar. Note that X1 has an additional emphatic personal­ pronoun,
, “to me,” while the Nippur witness N5 apparently­ had the epithet
, “king,” following the name of the god ­Enlil.

office:text.text:p: My comprehension of this paragraph hinges on the notion of mada as “frontier,” in this case referring to the area around Kazallu, which is the main issue of the letter. However, it is probable that even if this were the case at some moments of transmission, many Old Babylonian students and teachers would have understood it simply as “land.”

18. The term ­zag-gu/gú-la/lá has been translated in a variety of ways. S. N. Kramer, JAOS 69 (1949) 214, rendered it a “a­ kind of chair,” Å. Sjöberg, Nanna-Suen 63 n. 3, translated it as­ “Ehrenplatz,” i.e., “place of honor,” followed by W. Sallaberger, Kultische Kalender 176 n. 820, while G. Selz, FAOS 15/2, 556; SEL 13 (1996) 7 proposes “Gerätekammer,” an “equipment room.” Literary use of this term is rare; I know only UET 6/1 67: 23 (Nanše Hymn E) zag-gu-lá åà hi-li mah si-a ­i[m-m]i-in-dúr-uå, SLTNi 35 iii 13–14 (Dumuzi-Inana C1, segment C 13), where Dumuzi addresses Inana: é diÑir-Ñá-åè ì-x-nú-en, zag-gu-la diÑir-Ñá-ka­ dinanna mu-un-da-tuå-ù-dè-en.10
There are illegible signs underneath the verb, most probably an Akkadian gloss. Note especially Schooldays 53: é-a ù-mu-ni-in-ku4 zag-gu-la bí-in-tuå, “after (the teacher) entered the house, he was seated on a chair of honor.” Clearly, zag-gu-la is a sacred­ seat of some kind. In ED and Ur III economic texts it is written zag-gú-lá, e.g., PDT 1­200: 5, CT 32 27 ii 6, TrDr 5521: 6, YOS 4 226: 10, AUCT 3 413: 25,­ all in Ur III cultic contexts (Drehem, Umma, Garšana); for OB references to Ñiåbanåur ­zag-gu-la see D. Charpin, Archives 37–38. Note also­ Erimhuå VI 188 (MSL 17 86) zag-gu-la = sa-a-gu, that is sagû,­ “shrine, holy room in a temple,” (CAD S 26). While it seems that in literary texts one “sits” or “occupies” a zag-gu-la, Ur III economic texts tend to support the interpretation of the late Erimhuš, providing evidence that they were located in palaces and temples. Thus in AnOr 7 87: it is åà ≠é-gal, in Princeton 1 123: 7 åà é-gal uru an-na, in SET 66: 3 åà é […], in AUCT 3 413: 25–26 in the house of one Arbitum (undoubtedly the wife of the important general Hun-Habur). It would seem that rhetorically the future king intends to follow Ur III customs, and impose royal control over cultic activities from the highest religious offices to the smallest house and temple shrines.
The plural pronouns in this and the following line are somewhat problematical. They cannot refer to Dagan and Enlil, as the plural shifters are present in manuscripts that only mention the former.

20. The en, lú-mah, and ereå-diÑir are the highest cultic officials of the land, as distinct from the administrative temple organization functionaries such as saÑa and åabra (on the lú-mah, in many cases the highest priest of a deity, of the same rank as en and possibly higher than the ereå-diÑir, see P. Steinkeller, FS Kienast, 632–37). Most important, these were royal appointees, chosen the gods by means of omens, and Išbi-Erra is thus claiming divine sanction for his rule. The reading ereå rather than nin reluctantly follows Steinkeller, as it seems to have been generally accepted.

text:p.text:span: is uncertain in ­this context; one can read
. Note that N1 and possibly X4 this is followed my
but I am not aware of any other evidence pointing in this direction. On the difference between
42 (1973) 32. One could entertain a translation “even my
poorest citizens will celebrate their festivals.” Note that a technical use of­ the term is encountered in an Ur III letter-order (W.­W. Hallo,

office:text.text:p: 26. Two of the Nippur texts (N1 & N2) use the verb gub (with –ta-) in the sense of nasãæum, “to remove from office,” as in ŠAr1: 22 (2; note that in this text X1 shows the same variant). Apparently, this meaning created problems for some scribes (at least N4, X1, X3, X3), who simplified the verb (gub-bu) to bu, with a similar but more forceful meaning. It is, of course, equally possible that this is free variation, and that one cannot assume the primacy of either version of the verb.

text:p.text:span: All previous translations have rendered these­ lines as “He captured Zinnum, the ruler/governor of Subir, and­ plundered Hamazi.” Although the traditional interpretation should not be­ completely ruled out, the present rendition is based on the­ verbal morphology of these and the preceding lines. In lines 30–33 the verbs­ all begin with the negative focus prefix
which signifies that the agent of­ the action (Iåbi-Erra) is not mentioned in the clause. In lines 34–35 the verbal prefix changes to

), and one must­ assume that Zinnum is the agent of these lines. One would expect­ an ergative ending on the agent but that is not always necessary­ with proper names; the author of X1, who seems to try to make sense of things that are unclear to him, provides Zinnum with an ergative marker. The unreliable scribe of N1 may have understood the line in the same manner as most modern renditions. Note that
has to be interpreted as a locative (missing only in N1), and not as a possessive form, hence “in Subir;” this is supported by the locative prefix
has been read in a variety of ways; the presently attested readings are
(for the first two see P.­ Steinkeller,
In the first reading the second consonant is
. While in lexical texts the word is usually written as
, in literary texts and in royal inscriptions it is encountered as
272, Samsuiluna 6 46. It is clear that the final A is not a grammatical ending but is originally a gloss that became part of the logogram. On the other hand, syllabic writings indicate that
; in such instances it is often followed by the gloss
. The difference in meaning is possibly that of “captive, prisoner” (

office:text.text:p: The three Nippur manuscripts that preserve the last sign have dab5 (N1, N3, N7) as the verb; this is attested only once, in the inscription of Šulgi cited above, and seems to be the earlier usage. The tradition represented by X1 and X3 bears witness to a hypercorrection based on the common OB literary use of åaÑa…ak (but dab5 in the royal inscriptions of Šulgi and Samsuiluna). The ever resourceful scribe of X1 then changed the verb ak of the next line to lah5 to avoid repetition of the same word, in accordance with the standard aesthetics of Sumerian literature.

Some teachers had difficulties with these lines, and apparently did not know if Hamazi was a place (GN) or a personal name (PN). Three of the four Nippur manuscripts treat it as a PN (N3, N5, N7) and omit the postfixed place name classifier /ki/, using instead of the prefixed PN classifier (broken in N7); one should therefore really translate Nippur “version” as “he took Mr. Hamazi prisoner.” The undependable writer of N1 solved the problem by omitting the line altogether. Since Hamazi is mentioned as a GN in line 9, it seems to indicate a lack of attention on the part of the students. The Sippar, Ur, and unprovenianced sources treat it as a GN. Ur2 and X3, which are often in agreement with each other in opposition to most other sources of this letter, have the line after 33, so that this event is ascribed by them to Išbi-Erra rather than to Zinnum.

36. Note the variant Nur-Ea (Si1, X1) for Nur-aæi—properly­ Nur-aæum—the historically attested ruler of Eånunna. This may ­suggest that the name Ea was still read with an initial /æ/ or­/ḥ/ by some in this period but it could also be a clue as to the place in which these manuscripts were written. See above, p. 000. The form nu-úr-a-æi is now documented in the Ur III text TCCP 2 35: 7 (Nippur, ŠS5.-.-).

39. ki-ni-åè gur is otherwise unattested. It has always been rendered as “he returned to their places,” but in that case one might expect ki-ne-ne-åè (is this a calque from Akkadian ana ašrīšu/ašriš utīršunūti?). Note also the third person marker on the verb, which is consistent in all manuscripts, should refer to the various governors as subjects, not objects. It is quite possible that for some this was the understanding but students obviously had problems with the long span of text back to the referent, Išbi-Erra. The scribe of X1 used gúr, “to bow down in obeisance,” possibly trying to make sense of all this.

text:p.text:span: . The metaphor used here, and its echo in line 47, recalls
, “Her loud clamor makes the gods of the country tremble, her scream makes the Anuna quiver like a single reed.” This is the only other occurrence of
in Sumerian; in Akkadian, compare Enlil’s words in
II 7 (W. G. Lambert and A. R. Millard,
, “the ‘noise’ of humanity had become oppressive to me.”
and translated “Iåbierra stood at the head of his army,” as did S. N. Kramer,
334. The reduplication of the­ intransitive root with a singular subject implies habitual­ action.
. There is more confusion and deviation in these lines than in any other passage in this letter, suggesting interpretive confusion in antiquity.

. The personal name at the beginning of the line is problematical, compounded by the fact that the city of Malgium is not attested prior to OB times. All previous renditions interpret it as Iddin-Malgium, or the like, a name that would be highly improbable in an Ur III context. The matter is further complicated by the wide variety of writings of the name of the city attested in the Old Babylonian period. One scribe, who wrote N4, put Idi and Malgium on separate lines; it may be that others interpreted it as the personal name is Iddin-Malgium, although this was clearly not well understood by some of scribes (X1 solved it with a hypocoristic Iddiluma). The verbal form
must be transitive here ­(see the discussion of Ur III
; if the name is Iddin-Malgium, then the verb has no direct object and the locative has no referent. Malgium, of course, is unattested in Ur III documents; see p.
is otherwise encountered only in martial contexts in the inscriptions of Hammurabi and Samsuiluna and in one Inana hymn (M. Jaques,
147) and is usually translated as “to hate” (Akkadian
) but here, as in the inscriptions, it is used in the sense “to resist, rebel;” see, e.g., Samsuiluna 8: 36 [E4.3.7.8, Frayne
390]) and Samsuiluna YD 14. The non-Nippur texts X1 and X2 as well as N4 have the quasi-synonym
(see note to line 1 above), with the meaning “opposed him.”
There is some question as to the subject of the three­ verbs in the sentence. I take Girbubu to be the agent of­
, with the switch of ­reference indicated by the independent pronoun

. More­ problematical is the matter of the agents in these lines, an issue that obviously also caused problems for ancient students, as exemplified by the erroneous first or second person ending in N1 (“I/you resisted”); indeed all nine witnesses to this line have completely different forms of the initial predicate.

office:text.text:p: It is usually assumed that Iåbi-Erra was ­the one who instigated the action symbolized by the “cutting” ­of Girbubu’s shield. The grammar, such as it is, suggests that Girbubu ­did the cutting, to symbolize his resistence to the man imposed by Išbi-Erra, and that the­ independent pronoun marks the switch reference to the latter, who imposed his vengence. However, in the reply Ibbi-Sin demands action of both Puzur-Numušda and Girbubu.

text:p.text:span: For the reading as well as the meaning of
in N4; most likely, it is a clever phonetic variant, similar to those discussed by M. Civil,
and reinterpreted the phrase as “he emptied his grain stores.”
52, translated these lines as: “He ­has fixed his eyes upon me, I have no ally (and) no one to go ­with.” The translation of the last verb was based on a reading­
, “to be equal to, to compare with, to vie with.”

office:text.text:p: This use of igi…Ñar with the meaning “turn attention to” is also encountered in Curse of Agade 222–224: mìn-kam-ma-åè dsuºen den-ki dinana dnin-urta diåkur dutu dnuska dnidaba diÑir hé-em-me-eå, uruki-åè igi-ne-ne i-im-Ñá-Ñá-ne, a-ga-dèki áå hul-a im-ma-ab-bal-e-ne, “Then again Sin, Enki, Inana, Ninurta, Iškur, Utu, Nuska, Nidaba, the gods who were (there), turned their attention to Agade, and cursed it.” Note that lines 48–49 are quoted in the answer from Ibbi-Sin ­to Puzur-Numuåda (IbP1: 8–9, Letter 24).

text:p.text:span: 32 [1973] 126) has observed, this ­is one of only two attestations of a negative verbal form in subordinate clauses of the type
. He notes the incongruence of negation and a temporal­ clause, and suggests the causative meaning “since.”

office:text.text:p: Sources

text:note-body.text:p: In SL labeled as 3N-T 919,959 (in list of sources as 3N-T 919), in RCU as 3N-T919,486.
CBS 6987 also STVC 98, N 3603 also SL xliii; Sumer 26 175.
Obverse and reverse to be reversed.
Obverse and reverse to be reversed.
The surface of this tablet is very worn in parts and extremely difficult to read. The interpretations offered here are the result of multiple collations.

office:text.text:p: Si1 = Si 524 (Copy F. Geers) 6–16; 35–46
X4 = Cornell 63 19–44
N1collated from a photograph. Su1 could not be located. X3 collated by P. Steinkeller.

X1 possibly from Sippar; X3 possibly from Ur. X4 was identified by Alhena Gadotti, who kindly transliterated it and arranged for photographs (by Lisa Kinney-Bajwa), just as this book was being finished. I was not able to study the tablet in person. The tablet is extremely worn. N9 was also identified at the last minute by Manfred Krebernik, who kindly provided a transliteration and digital photo.
Tablet typology: compilation tablets (Type I): N1, N5, Ur1, X1, X2. All the rest are Type III, except Su1, which is a Type IV lentil.

text:p.text:span: N1 oo o .. . . + o o o o o
Ur2 oo . .. . o o o o o o o
. o oo o o o o o o o o

text:section.text:p: N1 .. . o o o o o o o o + . . . o o

text:p.text:span: N8 oo o . . + . o o o o o o . . o o
o o o o o/ o . . . . o
N1 + + + + ++ + . o o o o o o
N8 o o o o oo o o o . . o o o
o o oo o o o o o o o o
N4 oo . o o o o o o o o o o o o o o
N6 oo o . . . o o o o o o o o o o o
N8 ++ + . . o o . + + . o o o o o o
N6 .. . + . . o o o o o o o o o
N8 .+ + + . o o o o + + o o o o
Si1 o. + + + . o o o . . o o o o
X3 .. . . . . o o o o o o o o o

text:section.text:p: 7. gú ídidigna gú ídburanun-na gú ídabgal ù gú ídme-den-líl-lá

text:p.text:span: o + + . . o oo oo o o
N6 o .. o oo o o o o o . oo oo o o
N8 o .+ . oo o / o . . o o oo oo o o

text:note-body.text:p: Although this source utilizes the writing with final na in line 43, there does not seem to be enough space here for this sign (UD.≠KIB.NUN).

text:p.text:span: - + . o o oo oo o o
N6 o o o o o . o o o o o
N6 o o o o . o o o o o o o o o oo o
Ur1 must have had more after ≠
. . . . o o o o o oo o
Si1 + + + + . . + + . o o o o o oo o :
+ + + + + o o o o o o o oo o
N5 o o o o o o + ++ o o o o o o + šè + + x

text:note-body.text:p: The surface of N5 is extremely worn at this point (lines 11–21). To simplify matters, whenever a sign can be even vaguely identified, it is marked as +; only non-broken signs at the ends of lines are notated as . or x.

text:section.text:p: N5 o o o o o o o + + + .

text:note-body.text:p: Written as dib.

text:section.text:p: X1 o o o o . + + . e

text:note-body.text:p: Written as MU!
Possibly the scribe began to write hé and then continued with sá.

text:p.text:span: N5 + + + +. o o o o o o o
x o oo o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: The remnants of two signs at the beginning of the line cannot be deciphered; it is impossible to determine if the line was split over two lines or not.

text:section.text:p: N5 + + + + + + + . o o o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: Misshapen; liiks more like um.

text:p.text:span: X1 o o o o o . + o + . . .

text:note-body.text:p: Possibly tuš.

text:section.text:p: X4 o o . . . o o o o

text:p.text:span: N5 + + + + + . o o o o o o
N8 o o o o . o o o . + . o

text:note-body.text:p: Preceded by an erased ne.

text:section.text:p: N5 o + + . . o o o o o
X2 o o o o o o o o o .

text:p.text:span: Ur2 o oo o o o . o . -
X2 o oo o o o o . . -

text:note-body.text:p: Probably an erased sign; it appears that the sudent began to write la.

text:p.text:span: N2 o oo o o o o . .
Ur2 o oo o . o o . ta
N4 o o o o o . . .
Ur2 o o o o . . b[í-ib]-
X2 o o o o o . + +

text:note-body.text:p: It is possible that there was a sign between ni and sízkur.

text:p.text:span: Ur2 . o o o o o o o

X2 o o o o o o o o :

Ur1 šà + + . . . o o o o o

X1 o o o o o o x + + +

X2 o o o o o o + + + + +

text:section.text:p: N2 o o o o o o
N2 o o o o o o . o o .
N2 o o o o o o o o o o .

text:note-body.text:p: It may be that the scribe began to write te and then decided to use di.

text:p.text:span: o o o o o o o
X2 o o o o o o o . . + +

text:note-body.text:p: The sign looks more like lú.

text:section.text:p: N6 . + + + . . o o o o o o o
X2 o o o o o o o o x + + + +
N4 o oo o . + + / o o . + +
N6 o o. + + + + . o o o o

text:p.text:span: N7 o oo o o o . . o o o o
X2 o oo o o o o o o + + +

text:note-body.text:p: Written in all preserved cases as LÚ≈GÁNA-tenû.A; see comment to lines 34­35.

text:section.text:p: N4 oo o o o . / o o o o o o o o o
N6 oo o . . . . . o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: There does not seem to be room for ki; note that the scribe omits the ki in the next line as well, but writes it in line 36.

text:section.text:p: Ur2 omits, or had after line 35

text:p.text:span: X2 oo o o o o . . . . . . . .
o o o o o o(after 35)

text:section.text:p: X4 o. - . . . . . o o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: Si1 + + . . . o o o o o o

text:section.text:p: Si1 ++ + é-a . o o o o (after 37)
X3 - +. .+ + o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: X4 - o. .+ . o o o x o o :
N7 o o o o o
X3 . + . + + ta + + . o o ì . o

text:section.text:p: N4 oo o o o / o . o o o o o
N7 oo o o o o o . + + + +
X4 o o o o o o :
43. gú ídidigna gú ídburanun-na gú ídabgal ù gú ídme-den-líl-lá ­ba-an-dab5

text:note-body.text:p: Written UD.NUN.

text:p.text:span: X2 o oo o o. ki + +áb-gal . + ++ ++ + + + + +
X4 . .+ . .. ki / o o o o o oo oo + - + + +

text:section.text:p: X2 oo o o o o o o + + + +
X4 oo o o o o o o o . . .

text:p.text:span: N2 oo o o . . o o o
X2 oo o o o o o o . :
N2 o o o o o o o . . . . . - - - - - -

text:note-body.text:p: Perhaps the end of the line continued around the edge.

text:p.text:span: o .o o o o o + + . o o o

text:section.text:p: X2 . in-da-ak-e / oo o o o o o . + + + +
N2 o o o o o . + +

text:p.text:span: N2 o o o o o + + +
X3 . x x . x x x x

text:section.text:p: N2 o o o o o o . + + + + +
N4 . + . o o / - + . o o o o
N5 . + + + o o o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: N2 o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: Sign looks more like the beginning of da rather than of un. There is no room for both.
Reading of line uncertain.

text:p.text:span: N2 o o o o o o . + o o . + +
